hero.title: HearthCode
hero.subtitle: Pick Ember or Moss, preview the shipped theme live, then install it where you already work.
hero.quick.label: Quick pick
hero.quick.climateHint: Then choose the version inside it.
hero.selection.label: Now previewing
hero.quick.status.available: This style is in the current public package.
hero.quick.status.preview: This style is preview-only on the site for now.
hero.quick.real: All swatches here are pulled from the shipped theme files.
hero.renderNote.prefix: Site preview is calibrated to the shipped theme; open
hero.renderNote.suffix: for the final editor rendering before install.
hero.cta.hint: See the system first. Pick the version next. Install on your surface last.
hero.choice.flavor.title: Style
hero.choice.flavor.body: This is the authored palette direction: each style should feel like its own visual order at first glance.
hero.choice.climate.title: Variant
hero.choice.climate.body: This is the version inside that style. Dark and light can evolve freely as long as the style still feels coherent.
hero.choice.surface.title: Surface
hero.choice.surface.body: This is only the delivery target: VS Code, VSX-compatible editors, or Obsidian.
proof.label: Design Preview
proof.title: Start with the look, not the documentation.
proof.subtitle: HearthCode ships two palette directions across dark, light, and soft variants. The fastest path is to pick the family first, then refine the pressure around it.
proof.metric.1.label: Ember for warm paper, Moss for drier paper
proof.metric.2.label: the quickest Ember and Moss dark/light entry points
proof.metric.3.label: VS Code, Open VSX editors, and Obsidian
proof.compare.caption: The preview below shows the current released family. If you want the shortest path, start with one of these four picks.
proof.variant.dark.title: HearthCode Ember Dark
proof.variant.dark.body: Warm default with clear hierarchy and restrained denim-blue function anchors.
proof.variant.soft.title: HearthCode Ember Dark Soft
proof.variant.soft.body: Lower-stimulus dark variant with softened chroma while preserving role separation.
proof.variant.light.title: HearthCode Ember Light
proof.variant.light.body: Paper-toned light mode with warm foundation and controlled cool callable anchors.
proof.variant.lightSoft.title: HearthCode Ember Light Soft
proof.variant.lightSoft.body: Gentler light contrast for long daytime sessions, with separation gates kept intact.
proof.cta.hint: Choose the editor you use today and install from its official channel.
platform.label: Selection Guide
platform.title: Know what you are choosing
platform.subtitle: There are only three decisions on this site. Once those are clear, the rest of the page becomes much easier to read.
platform.choice.flavor.name: Flavor changes the palette
platform.choice.flavor.body: Choose a flavor when you want a different overall color family. This is the part that should feel noticeably different at first glance.
platform.choice.climate.name: Climate changes the pressure
platform.choice.climate.body: Choose a climate when the palette already feels right but you want it darker, lighter, softer, or more defined for your environment.
platform.choice.surface.name: Surface changes the delivery
platform.choice.surface.body: Choose the surface last. It should not change the color language itself, only which app or editor receives it.
platform.item.vsx.name: Open VSX Ecosystem
platform.item.vsx.body: Open VSX-compatible editors consume the same generated role map and palette behavior.
platform.item.vscode.name: VS Code Marketplace
platform.item.vscode.body: VS Code package is built from the same semantic source without platform-only color forks.
platform.item.obsidian.name: Obsidian App Theme
platform.item.obsidian.body: Obsidian app-theme inherits the same semantic language through mapped CSS variables.
platform.item.next.name: Next Surfaces
platform.item.next.body: Additional adapters are planned on top of the same semantic source contract.
philosophy.label: HearthCode Philosophy
philosophy.01.title: Ember, not flame
philosophy.01.body: Hearth keeps highlight colors in a controlled saturation band first, then enforces role separation thresholds so syntax remains readable before style polish.
philosophy.02.title: Neither black nor white
philosophy.02.body: Pure black backgrounds can create halation around white text and blur edges. Hearth uses warm, non-extreme background tones across all four variants to keep glare down and reading layers stable.
philosophy.03.title: Hierarchy as semantics
philosophy.03.body: Hierarchy is semantic and measurable: role contrast and deltaE gates are enforced so keywords, functions, methods, and variables stay distinguishable in long sessions.
philosophy.04.title: Color temperature as comfort
philosophy.04.body: The system stays warm by default. Cool hues are used as small structural anchors (mainly callable roles), not as a global temperature shift.
colors.label: Color System
colors.role.bg.dark: Dark canvas 鈥?soot blackboard beside the hearth
colors.role.bg.darkSoft: Dark Soft canvas 鈥?warmer gray-black for lower night contrast stress
colors.role.fg.dark: Dark foreground 鈥?chalky parchment white for long focus
colors.role.keyword: Keywords 鈥?ember red for control flow anchors
colors.role.function: Functions 鈥?denim blue for deliberate callable anchors
colors.role.string: Strings 鈥?moss green with warm base
colors.role.type: Types 鈥?dark ochre for structural symbols
colors.role.comment: Comments 鈥?intentionally dim, eye skips naturally
colors.role.bg.light: Light background 鈥?parchment off-white to reduce glare
colors.role.bg.lightSoft: Light Soft background 鈥?softer parchment tone for calmer daylight contrast
colors.role.fg.light: Light foreground 鈥?deep walnut brown for long-form reading
colors.role.fg.lightSoft: Light Soft foreground 鈥?mellow walnut ink to keep contrast pressure low
colors.proof.title: Built from source
colors.proof.body: These swatches come from the shipped tokens and release checks.
preview.label: Live Token Preview
fit.label: Theme Picker
fit.title: Then choose the exact version you want to use
fit.subtitle: Every card below is built from the shipped theme files. First choose the palette family, then choose the pressure around it.
fit.step.flavor.title: 1. Pick the palette family
fit.step.flavor.body: Choose the version whose overall color personality feels right at a glance.
fit.step.climate.title: 2. Pick the climate around it
fit.step.climate.body: Once the family is right, decide whether you want it darker, lighter, softer, or more defined.
fit.result.placeholder: Answer a few questions to see your fit
fit.result.placeholderBody: We will recommend a starting flavor and climate, plus a backup option you can compare in the live preview.
fit.result.publishedBody: This flavor is part of the current public package, so you can carry the recommendation straight into install.
fit.result.previewBody: This flavor is visible in the site preview while we evaluate whether it belongs in the public package.
fit.question.lighting.title: What lighting do you usually work in?
fit.question.lighting.option.daylight: Bright daylight or strong office light
fit.question.fatigue.title: What kind of theme fatigue bothers you most?
fit.question.fatigue.option.balanced: I mostly want a balanced default
fit.question.tone.title: Which overall tone sounds closer to you?
fit.question.tone.option.either: Either, if the hierarchy is good
fit.question.workflow.title: What do you spend more time on?
fit.question.hierarchy.title: How much structure do you like on screen?
fit.climate.dark.label: Dark
fit.climate.dark.summary: Dark is the balanced mixed-light default when you want stronger hierarchy without pushing into harsh contrast.
fit.climate.darkSoft.label: Dark Soft
fit.climate.darkSoft.summary: Dark Soft lowers pressure for dim rooms and long late sessions while keeping the role map intact.
fit.climate.light.label: Light
fit.climate.light.summary: Light is the daylight pick when documents and bright rooms matter as much as code.
fit.climate.lightSoft.label: Light Soft
fit.climate.lightSoft.summary: Light Soft is the gentlest daytime option when you want paper-like calm over sharp contrast.
final.label: Install
final.title: Install or Star
final.subtitle: Use the package you want right now, or jump to GitHub if you want to follow the project.
final.option.vscode.title: VS Code Marketplace
final.option.vscode.body: For the official VS Code Marketplace install.
final.option.openvsx.title: Open VSX Editors
final.option.openvsx.body: For Cursor, Windsurf, VSCodium, and other Open VSX-compatible editors.
final.option.obsidian.title: Obsidian Theme
final.option.obsidian.body: For notes and long-form reading in Obsidian with the same HearthCode direction.
final.option.github.title: GitHub
final.option.github.body: Follow releases, file issues, or star the project.
final.option.docs.title: Baseline Docs
final.option.docs.body: For the baseline and source docs.
final.meta.secondary: 2 directions 路 8 themes 路 3 surfaces
footer.tagline: Color Language for Code Interfaces 路 2026
proof.guide.title: Four strong starting points
proof.guide.dark.title: HearthCode Ember Dark
proof.guide.dark.body: Start here if you want the default warm direction for mixed light and long coding sessions.
proof.guide.darkSoft.title: HearthCode Moss Dark
proof.guide.darkSoft.body: Start here if you want a drier dark theme with cleaner cuts and sharper syntax landmarks.
proof.guide.light.title: HearthCode Ember Light
proof.guide.light.body: Start here if you want a warm paper light theme for bright rooms and docs-heavy work.
proof.guide.lightSoft.title: HearthCode Moss Light
proof.guide.lightSoft.body: Start here if you want a cleaner paper light theme with a more structural, editorial feel.
platform.item.core.name: Core Color System
platform.item.core.body: Semantic roles, variant routing, and tuning live in color-system/* and generate downstream artifacts.
